senses.definition: Having functional breathing openings at both the front and back of the body, used especially for some insect larvae in biology.
examples: The larvae of this fly are amphipneustic and breathe through spiracles at each end of the body.
In amphipneustic larvae, only the front and rear spiracles are well developed.
The researcher described the amphipneustic respiratory system in detail.

senses.definition: Breathing both in water and in air, used mainly of certain fishes and other aquatic animals in scientific writing.
examples: The amphipnous fish can survive in muddy pools during the dry season.
Some amphipnous species use special organs to breathe air directly.
The ecology of amphipnous vertebrates is still being studied.

senses.definition: A small shrimp-like crustacean with many legs, found mainly in water and sometimes on land.
examples: The biologist collected several amphipod species from the river bed.
Amphipod populations are often used as indicators of water quality.
Fish in this lake feed heavily on tiny amphipods.

senses.definition: A large group (order) of crustaceans that includes amphipods, with a body that is flattened from side to side.
examples: Amphipoda is a diverse order of mainly aquatic crustaceans.
The class included an introduction to Amphipoda and Isopoda.
Species in Amphipoda occupy many different ecological niches.

senses.definition: Relating to or belonging to amphipods or the order Amphipoda.
examples: The amphipodal fauna of the cave has been little studied.
She wrote a paper on amphipodal morphology.
Amphipodal species are abundant in this estuary.

senses.definition: Relating to amphipods or the order Amphipoda.
examples: The amphipodan community was dominated by a single species.
Amphipodan diversity increases towards the poles.
They compared amphipodan assemblages from different depths.

senses.definition: An animal that belongs to the group of amphipods.
examples: This amphipodan is common in shallow coastal waters.
Several amphipodans were found living among the algae.
The specimen was identified as an Antarctic amphipodan.

senses.definition: Having a body shape similar to that of an amphipod.
examples: The larvae are amphipodiform and later change into a different form.
Amphipodiform bodies are well adapted to life between sand grains.
The crustacean has an amphipodiform appearance.

senses.definition: Relating to or having the form of an amphipod.
examples: The amphipodous crustaceans were found under stones.
An amphipodous body plan is common in this group.
Amphipodous forms dominate the invertebrate community here.

senses.definition: The plural of amphipod: small shrimp-like crustaceans with many legs, mostly living in water.
examples: Amphipods form an important part of the diet of many fish.
The scientist measured how pollution affected the amphipods.
Amphipods can be seen swimming quickly among the plants.

senses.definition: Having rows of columns at both ends, especially of a classical temple or similar building.
examples: The amphiprostylar temple design was common in ancient Greece.
They studied the proportions of several amphiprostylar buildings.
An amphiprostylar layout gives the structure a balanced look.

senses.definition: Having a row of columns at the front and back but not along the sides, especially of a classical temple.
examples: The small amphiprostyle temple stood on a low hill overlooking the sea.
An amphiprostyle building has porticoes only at its ends.
The architect chose an amphiprostyle form for the memorial.

senses.definition: A temple or building with columns at the front and back but not at the sides.
examples: The best-known amphiprostyle in the city is the temple by the river.
As an amphiprostyle, the structure lacks side colonnades.
Several amphiprostyles were built during the same period.

senses.definition: The plural of amphiprostyle: temples or buildings with columns at the front and back but not at the sides.
examples: Few amphiprostyles from the period have survived.
The guide pointed out several amphiprostyles in the ancient sanctuary.
Architects compared amphiprostyles with peripteral temples.

senses.definition: Able to act either as an acid or as a base by giving or taking a proton, used in chemistry.
examples: Water is an amphiprotic substance that can donate or accept a proton.
Amphiprotic solvents are common in acid–base reactions.
The ion is amphiprotic and can react in two different ways.

senses.definition: A substance found around the nucleus in some cells, mentioned mainly in older biological literature.
examples: The dye stained the amphipyrenin surrounding the nucleus.
Early researchers believed amphipyrenin played a role in cell division.
The presence of amphipyrenin was noted in the micrographs.

senses.definition: A condition or type of skull having two separate nasal openings, used in anatomical description.
examples: Amphirhina is typical of many vertebrate skulls.
The fossil shows clear amphirhina.
Researchers used the presence of amphirhina as a diagnostic feature.

senses.definition: Having two separate nasal openings, used in anatomical description.
examples: The species has an amphirhinal skull.
Amphirhinal structures are common in many mammals.
They compared amphirhinal and single-nostril forms.

senses.definition: Having two distinct nostrils or nasal passages, as in most vertebrates.
examples: Humans are amphirhine mammals.
The amphirhine condition allows separate nasal airflow.
Amphirhine skulls are contrasted with single-nostril forms.

senses.definition: A large tropical fruit with a hard outer shell and juicy pulp inside, related to garcinias, mentioned mainly in botanical texts.
examples: The tree produces amphisarca with thick woody rinds.
Amphisarca are eaten fresh or used in local dishes.
They studied the seeds of amphisarca for germination rates.

senses.definition: A mythical snake-like creature with a head at each end of its body.
examples: The legend tells of an amphisbaena that could move in either direction.
Medieval writers described the amphisbaena as a fearsome serpent.
The artist painted an amphisbaena coiled around the tree.

senses.definition: A member of a group of small, legless, worm-like lizards that live mostly underground in warm regions.
examples: The biologist found an amphisbaena beneath the stone.
Amphisbaena species are adapted to life in soil.
They compared the skulls of amphisbaena and other lizards.

senses.definition: The plural of amphisbaena, referring either to two-headed mythical serpents or to worm-like lizards of the genus Amphisbaena.
examples: Stories of amphisbaenae appear in several ancient sources.
The collection contained preserved amphisbaenae from South America.
Illustrations showed amphisbaenae twisting in opposite directions.

senses.definition: The regular English plural of amphisbaena, used for either the mythical creature or the real worm lizards.
examples: Drawings of amphisbaenas often exaggerate their two heads.
Amphisbaenas are found mainly in tropical regions.
Writers sometimes use amphisbaenas as symbols of duality.

senses.definition: Relating to amphisbaenas or to the group of legless, burrowing reptiles that includes them.
examples: Amphisbaenian reptiles are highly specialized for digging.
The study focused on amphisbaenian anatomy.
Amphisbaenian lineages have evolved independently.

senses.definition: A reptile that belongs to the group of legless, burrowing reptiles related to amphisbaenas.
examples: The amphisbaenian was captured in a sandy field.
Few people ever see an amphisbaenian in the wild.
They described a new amphisbaenian from the desert.

senses.definition: Relating to or having the characteristics of an amphisbaena, either mythical or real.
examples: The creature in the story has an amphisbaenic form.
Amphisbaenic features include a long, cylindrical body.
She explored amphisbaenic motifs in medieval art.

senses.definition: Belonging to the family Amphisbaenidae, a group of legless, burrowing lizards.
examples: Amphisbaenid reptiles are found in many tropical regions.
They described the skull of an amphisbaenid species.
Amphisbaenid forms show extreme adaptation to life underground.

senses.definition: A reptile that is a member of the family Amphisbaenidae.
examples: This amphisbaenid is rarely seen above ground.
The museum holds several amphisbaenids in its collection.
The amphisbaenid was preserved in alcohol for study.

senses.definition: A family of legless, burrowing lizards that includes many species of worm lizards.
examples: Amphisbaenidae contains numerous genera distributed worldwide.
The family Amphisbaenidae is characterized by elongated bodies.
He specialized in the taxonomy of Amphisbaenidae.

senses.definition: Resembling or related to amphisbaenas or worm lizards.
examples: The fossil shows amphisbaenoid features.
Amphisbaenoid reptiles share several anatomical traits.
They compared amphisbaenoid and snake-like forms.

senses.definition: Having the nature or appearance of an amphisbaena, especially having two similar ends.
examples: The poet described an amphisbaenous monster in the cave.
An amphisbaenous design shows symmetry at both ends.
The amphisbaenous creature seemed to face two ways at once.

senses.definition: Relating to places on Earth where shadows can fall either north or south at different times of the year, near the equator.
examples: The city lies in the amphiscian zone.
Amphiscian regions experience the sun directly overhead twice a year.
Ancient geographers wrote about amphiscian lands.

senses.definition: A person or region located where shadows sometimes fall to the north and sometimes to the south during the year.
examples: The islanders are amphiscian, living close to the equator.
Ancient texts mention amphiscians and periscians.
The amphiscian lives in a place where the sun passes overhead.

senses.definition: People or regions where shadows fall both north and south at different times of year, near the equator.
examples: Ancient writers called the inhabitants of equatorial lands amphiscians.
Amphiscians experience the sun at the zenith twice a year.
The map marked the zones of amphiscians and heteroscians.

senses.definition: Regions of the Earth where shadows at noon sometimes fall to the north and sometimes to the south during the year, that is, the equatorial belt.
examples: The amphiscii lie between the two tropics.
Ancient geographers divided the world into amphiscii and heteroscii.
Climates in the amphiscii are generally warm.

senses.definition: A small tropical moth of the genus Amphisile, mentioned mainly in entomological works.
examples: An amphisile rested on the leaf during the day.
They recorded the presence of an amphisile in the forest.
The amphisile was attracted to the light trap.

senses.definition: A small family of moths that includes the genus Amphisile.
examples: Amphisilidae are found mainly in tropical regions.
The family Amphisilidae contains only a few species.
He specializes in the taxonomy of Amphisilidae.

senses.definition: Having seeds attached at both ends, used in specialized botanical description.
examples: The plant shows amphispermous fruits.
Amphispermous structures are rare in this family.
They described an amphispermous arrangement of seeds.

senses.definition: Producing spores in two kinds of sporangia or at two positions, used in descriptions of some plants and fungi.
examples: The fungus is amphisporangiate, with sporangia at both ends of the hyphae.
Amphisporangiate species may have complex life cycles.
They examined the amphisporangiate structures under the microscope.

senses.definition: A spore with two similar ends, or with special features at both ends, described in some fungi and lower plants.
examples: The fungus produces amphispores in late summer.
Each amphispore has thick walls at both ends.
They counted the number of amphispores per fruiting body.

senses.definition: A parasitic flatworm (fluke) that has suckers on both the front and back of its body.
examples: The veterinarian detected an amphistoma in the animal's intestine.
Amphistoma infestations can cause serious disease in livestock.
The life cycle of the amphistoma includes a snail host.

senses.definition: Having stomata (tiny openings for gas exchange) on both the upper and lower surfaces of a leaf.
examples: Many grasses have amphistomatal leaves.
Amphistomatal leaves can exchange gases efficiently.
They compared amphistomatal and hypostomatal species.

senses.definition: Having stomata on both sides of a leaf, used in plant anatomy.
examples: Amphistomatic leaves are common in plants growing in bright light.
The species is amphistomatic, with stomata distributed on both surfaces.
They classified the leaf as amphistomatic after microscopic examination.

senses.definition: A parasitic fluke that has suckers at both ends of its body.
examples: The amphistome attaches to the lining of the intestine.
Farmers treat cattle to prevent amphistome infections.
The life cycle of the amphistome involves aquatic snails.

senses.definition: Resembling an amphistome or having features similar to flukes with suckers at both ends.
examples: The parasite has an amphistomoid shape.
Amphistomoid forms are adapted to attach firmly to host tissues.
They described the worm as amphistomoid in structure.

senses.definition: Having suckers at both the front and back of the body, used especially of certain parasitic flukes.
examples: Amphistomous flukes attach strongly to their hosts.
The worm is amphistomous, with two distinct suckers.
They examined the amphistomous arrangement under the microscope.

senses.definition: A genus of parasitic flukes that have suckers at both ends of the body.
examples: Amphistomum species infect the digestive tract of cattle.
The veterinarian identified the parasite as Amphistomum.
Amphistomum has a complex life cycle involving snails.

senses.definition: Having rows of columns at two opposite sides, especially of a classical building.
examples: The amphistylar temple had colonnades on both fronts.
They documented several amphistylar structures in the ancient city.
An amphistylar plan balances the building's appearance.

senses.definition: Buildings or temples that are amphistylar, with columns at two opposite sides.
examples: Only a few amphistylars remain standing today.
The guide compared the amphistylars with other temple types.
These amphistylars show a similar architectural style.

senses.definition: Having the upper jaw attached to the skull in two places, described in some primitive sharks and fishes.
examples: Early sharks had amphistylic jaw suspension.
The amphistylic condition is considered primitive.
They illustrated the amphistylic arrangement in the diagram.

senses.definition: A type of jaw suspension in which the upper jaw is attached to the skull in two places, seen in some primitive fishes.
examples: Amphistyly occurs in early shark lineages.
The evolution from amphistyly to hyostyly is well documented.
They discussed the functional advantages of amphistyly.

senses.definition: Relating to a stage in meiosis where paired chromosomes first become visible, used in cytology.
examples: The cells were fixed at the amphitene stage.
Amphitene chromosomes appear thin and thread-like.
They photographed nuclei in amphitene for comparison.

senses.definition: The plural of amphithalamus, referring to paired inner chambers or cavities, especially in anatomical or zoological context.
examples: The skull shows well-developed amphithalami.
They measured the volume of the amphithalami.
Amphithalami are separated by a thin bony wall.

senses.definition: A paired inner chamber or cavity, especially one of the two main chambers in certain shells or anatomical structures.
examples: The shell has an amphithalamus divided into two parts.
They examined the amphithalamus under magnification.
Damage to the amphithalamus affected the organism's balance.

senses.definition: An alternative plural form of amphithalamus, referring to paired inner chambers or cavities in anatomical or zoological structures.
examples: The amphithalmi of the skull are clearly visible in the section.
They compared the size of the amphithalmi in different species.
Fluid filled the amphithalmi after the injury.
